chapters.title: Source trail & scope
chapters.content_preview: Source trail & scope        This handbook follows the Utpātādi-yoga-catuṣṭaya rule: for each weekday, four consecutive nakshatras produce Utpāta, Mṛtyu, Kāṇa, and Siddhi, respectively.  The clearest classical source trail is Muhūrta Cintāmaṇi, in the Śubhāśubha-prakaraṇa. The te

chapters.title: Vāra / WeekdayPlanetary lordUtpāta YogaMṛtyu YogaKāṇa YogaSiddhi Yoga
chapters.content_preview: Vāra / WeekdayPlanetary lordUtpāta YogaMṛtyu YogaKāṇa YogaSiddhi Yoga        Note: Monday’s Kāṇa entry uses Abhijit, the intercalary nakshatra placed between Uttarāṣāḍhā and Śravaṇa in the 28-nakshatra scheme.

chapters.title: Searchable lookup
chapters.content_preview: Searchable lookup    Type a weekday, planet, nakshatra, or yoga name. This is useful when a panchang tells you “Thursday + Mṛgaśīrṣā” and you want the yoga instantly.      All yogasUtpātaMṛtyuKāṇaSiddhi  Reset

chapters.title: How to apply this in a panchang
chapters.content_preview: How to apply this in a panchang      1. Identify the Vāra  Use the weekday as per the local panchang convention. In many traditional panchangs, Vāra is sunrise-to-sunrise rather than midnight-to-midnight, so local sunrise matters for edge cases.  2. Identify the Moon’s Nakshatra
